ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_03311069); no specific vegetable biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers biryani as a rice dish category; no vegetable-specific biryani code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for vegetable biryani or biryani as a food concept at this specificity.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of vegetable biryani weighing approximately 350 g, typically served with raita or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), composite rice-based vegetable dish reference; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked basmati rice and mixed vegetables for micronutrient estimation.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports a composite vegetable rice dish (pulao/biryani category) with per-100g values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, fibre, and sodium. Micronutrient values (iron, calcium, vitamin C, potassium) are cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked basmati rice (USDA FDC ID 169704) and mixed cooked vegetables to produce a weighted composite. Values represent a typical restaurant-style vegetable biryani with ghee, basmati rice, and mixed vegetables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; basmati rice cooked (GI 57) used as primary analogous anchor for vegetable biryani.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable biryani in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cooked basmati rice has a well-established GI of 57 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2021 and the University of Sydney GI Database. Vegetable biryani is predominantly basmati rice with vegetables, ghee, and spices; the vegetable and fat content moderately lowers the effective GI relative to plain rice. GI of 57 is used as a conservative estimate anchored to the basmati rice reference. Glycemic load per serving = (57 × 23.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 46.9 g GL for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate per serving = 82.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the cumulative sodium from biryani masala, added salt, and ghee can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high carbohydrate density (23.5 g per 100 g); a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 82 g of carbohydrate, which can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to 150–200 g, pair the dish with a low-GI side such as raita or salad, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the carbohydrate load from a full restaurant portion may exceed recommended per-meal carbohydrate targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium; the dish is vegetarian and contains beneficial dietary fibre and potassium from vegetables, partially offsetting cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose versions prepared with minimal ghee or vegetable oil, control portion size to limit saturated fat and sodium intake, and ensure the dish is accompanied by additional vegetables to increase fibre and potassium contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Biryani (also known as Veg Biryani or Sabzi Biryani) is a fragrant, layered Indian rice dish prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice with a medley of vegetables — typically potato, carrot, green beans, peas, and onion — along with whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf, and ground biryani masala. The dish is traditionally finished using the dum method, in which the pot is sealed and the rice and vegetables are slow-cooked together over low heat so that the steam infuses the grains with spice and aroma. Ghee or vegetable oil is used to sauté the vegetables and bloom the spices before layering. Saffron or food colouring is often added to give the rice its characteristic golden hue. The texture is fluffy and aromatic, with distinct grains of rice and tender vegetable pieces. The flavour profile is complex — warm, mildly spiced, and slightly savoury — with fresh coriander and mint leaves providing brightness. In Singapore, vegetable biryani is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, often accompanied by raita (yoghurt-based condiment) or a vegetable curry. Nutritionally, a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 12 g protein, 15 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index (GI 57) anchored to basmati rice, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to biryani masala and added salt. The vegetable content contributes dietary fibre, potassium, vitamin A, and vitamin C. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable when prepared without non-halal ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in veg biryani?
What is the GI of biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between biryani and pulao?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for vegetable biryani exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo at the level of a restaurant-style dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN composite vegetable rice dish data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for basmati rice and mixed vegetables. GI is estimated from the well-established basmati rice GI of 57 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the values are defensible and source-anchored but not derived from a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category, and vegetable biryani contains no significant vitamin D source. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully vegetarian dish containing no animal flesh or eggs; the trace amount from yoghurt/ghee is below detection threshold — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN vegetarian rice dish reference. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero; ghee contains naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid but negligible industrial trans fat; reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy ghee entry. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate anchored to cooked basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). No direct GI measurement for vegetable biryani was found in the approved GI database. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311069 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for vegetable biryani exists in either system. 7. The input food_name 'Garden Biryani (Veg)' from GrabFood Singapore is a menu-style name; canonical English name normalised to 'Vegetable Biryani'. 8. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Indian restaurant biryani portion in Singapore context.
